</imports>

<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Foundation tier with zero coupling is the ideal architectural position; any future feature that tempts an upward import (e.g. llm-client importing from rex for context) must be rejected — that dependency direction inverts the hierarchy.
[observation] [info] Token parsing is housed in llm-client (not in hench), which is architecturally correct — cost accounting belongs at the provider layer where token counts originate, not in the agent layer that consumes them.
[observation] [info] With 30 re-exported symbols in hench's llm-gateway.ts, llm-client's public surface is wide; a periodic audit to identify which exports are actually consumed would help trim the gateway and reduce accidental API surface.
[suggestion] [info] Add a CI-time check (or test assertion) that every symbol re-exported in hench/src/prd/llm-gateway.ts is referenced by at least one non-gateway file in the hench package. Unused gateway re-exports indicate either dead llm-client API or a hench refactor that forgot to prune the gateway — both are correctness signals worth surfacing automatically.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- 44 files at perfect cohesion (1.0) with zero coupling correctly positions llm-client as the foundation tier — nothing above it should leak into its internals.
- Supporting three provider backends (api-provider, cli-provider, codex-cli-provider) behind a unified create-client.ts entry point is a clean strategy pattern; adding a fourth vendor requires only a new provider file and a factory branch.
- The auth.ts and exec.ts utilities are kept separate from provider logic, which supports testability — auth and execution concerns can be mocked independently.
- Foundation tier with zero coupling is the ideal architectural position; any future feature that tempts an upward import (e.g. llm-client importing from rex for context) must be rejected — that dependency direction inverts the hierarchy.
- With 30 re-exported symbols in hench's llm-gateway.ts, llm-client's public surface is wide; a periodic audit to identify which exports are actually consumed would help trim the gateway and reduce accidental API surface.
- Token parsing is housed in llm-client (not in hench), which is architecturally correct — cost accounting belongs at the provider layer where token counts originate, not in the agent layer that consumes them.
- llm-client exports 30+ symbols through hench's llm-gateway.ts but has no corresponding usage-coverage tracking — there is no mechanism to detect when a re-exported symbol in llm-gateway.ts is no longer consumed by any hench file. Dead re-exports in the gateway create false signals about what llm-client's public API actually serves, and accumulate silently after hench refactors.
- Add a CI-time check (or test assertion) that every symbol re-exported in hench/src/prd/llm-gateway.ts is referenced by at least one non-gateway file in the hench package. Unused gateway re-exports indicate either dead llm-client API or a hench refactor that forgot to prune the gateway — both are correctness signals worth surfacing automatically.
- [call graph] 494 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
